steps.title: Find the growth factor for a 3-unit change
steps.content:
In an exponential function $f(x) = ab^{\frac{x}{n}}$, ratios eliminate the constant $a$. Divide the two known values:
$$\frac{f(5)}{f(2)} = \frac{ab^{\frac{5}{n}}}{ab^{\frac{2}{n}}} = b^{\frac{3}{n}}$$
$$b^{\frac{3}{n}} = \frac{162}{6} = 27$$
This is the multiplicative growth when the input increases by 3.

steps.title: Scale to find the 2-unit growth factor
steps.content:
To go from $f(5)$ to $f(7)$, the input increases by 2 units. Rewrite the exponent $\frac{2}{n}$ in terms of what we know:
$$b^{\frac{2}{n}} = \left(b^{\frac{3}{n}}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}} = 27^{\frac{2}{3}}$$
$$27^{\frac{2}{3}} = (3^3)^{\frac{2}{3}} = 3^2 = 9$$

steps.title: Compute f(7) using the growth factor
steps.content:
Multiply $f(5)$ by the growth factor for a 2-unit change:
$$f(7) = f(5) \cdot b^{\frac{2}{n}} = 162 \cdot 9 = 1458$$
